steps.title: Identify the sentence's grammatical structure
steps.content: The sentence has a compound subject connected by “and”: his energetic gestures announcing when a player had struck out and his habit of barreling after a hit ball to see if it would land out of ______. A main verb must follow to govern both subjects and show their effect on the umpire role. The blank falls within the subject phrase, right before the verb that will complete the clause.

steps.title: Match the correct verb structure
steps.content:
With bounds helped, the sentence reads: “His gestures announcing… and his habit… out of bounds helped transform the role.” Here, helped is the main verb, and transform is an infinitive object (a standard construction: “helped transform” = “helped [to] transform”). This creates a complete, grammatically sound clause: the compound subject performed the action “helped,” with the outcome “transform the role.”

With bounds, helping, the comma would make “helping transform” a participial phrase, but no clear main verb follows—the sentence becomes incomplete. Bounds that helped introduces a relative clause with an ambiguous antecedent, and bounds to help doesn't fit the sentence's grammatical flow.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
